title: Dev-browser panel-embed boundary — direct import vs iframe/web-component mount for plateau-app's panels
summary: Prep research for decision #1654 (resolving #141 Fork 4-A, unblocking the #1391 dev-browser-shell slice). The Chromium dev-browser shell is Plateau-owned (#1565 devtool-placement-constellation: an operated-surface-against-your-own-build lives in Plateau), and the panels it must surface — the Technical Configurator, Intent Configurator and Profiles — ALREADY ship in the SAME repo as plain DOM mount functions: mountTechnicalConfigurator(root: HTMLElement) (plateau:src/technical-configurator/configurator.ts:639), mountIntentConfigurator(root) (plateau:src/intent-configurator/configurator.ts:421), mountProfiles(el) (plateau:src/profiles/profiles-page.ts:165) — no React/Vue, one Vite bundle (plateau:package.json has no react/vue), all called same-bundle from the SPA (plateau:src/main.ts:437/481/527). The shell dir plateau:src/dev-browser/ already exists. LOAD-BEARING FINDING that reshapes the fork: the shell and the panels are BOTH plateau-app code, so a 'direct import' is a SAME-REPO, SAME-ORIGIN function call — it is NOT a cross-constellation edge and never touches the WE->FUI->plateau DAG (a backward DAG edge is a CODE import upstream; an intra-repo import within the product layer is none). This is the opposite of the two prior plateau-embed transport decisions, which were cross-origin BY CONSTRUCTION: #788/plateau-embed-seed-transport (a WE/FUI DOCS surface embeds a Plateau configurator across origins) and #887/plateau-creator-fui-switcher-transport (a Plateau creator iframe feeds a FUI switcher iframe) both FORCED iframe+postMessage because the host and panel were different origins/repos and same-origin DOM was unreadable. Here the host and panel are the same origin, so neither forcing condition holds and the iframe boundary is no longer mandatory. Prior-art survey of devtools-shell embedding: VS Code webviews are sandboxed iframes on a separate origin talking only over postMessage (chosen for UNTRUSTED third-party extension content); Chrome DevTools extension panels are iframe-sandboxed for the same isolation-of-untrusted reason; micro-frontend practice splits the same way — Module Federation / direct-import for a shared-runtime trusted shell (deep integration, shared bundle, synchronous data flow, no postMessage marshalling), iframe/web-component only when you need HARD isolation (untrusted code, independent origin/deploy, CSS/JS encapsulation). The crux: direct import = tight coupling, shared bundle, synchronous in-process data flow, zero transport; iframe/web-component = isolation boundary, independent origin/deploy, postMessage transport cost. FORK-EXISTENCE TEST result: import-vs-iframe does NOT survive as a single either/or for the FIRST-PARTY same-repo panels — the panels are trusted plateau-app code with no independent-deploy or isolation need, so direct import is the strict default and the iframe branch has no merit advantage here (the things that earned iframe in #788/#887 — cross-origin, untrusted, runtime-authored — are all absent). Fork 1 (recommended default: A direct package import / mount-function call, confidence High). What DOES survive is a narrow boundary mechanism: when the shell renders a THIRD-PARTY conformant app's OWN embedded panels (the dev-browser's whole premise is loading other people's apps), that surface IS cross-origin/untrusted and reuses the established iframe+origin-validated-postMessage transport — Fork 2 (recommended default: A reuse the existing FUI embed-host origin-validated message bus pattern — frontierui:embed/embed-host.ts:227 rejects any message whose event.origin != the frame's validated origin, contract frontierui:embed/contract.ts:85 EmbedEnvelope channel 'fui-embed' — rather than mint a new Protocol, confidence Med-High). So the real shape is NOT one fork but support-BOTH-by-locus: import for the shell's own first-party trusted panels, iframe+postMessage for the loaded third-party app surface — a configuration split by trust/origin, not a single ruling. Classification: NOT a new minted WE Protocol (the first-party case is an in-process function call with no swappable-vendor interop story; the third-party case reuses the existing fui-embed envelope, a documented bilateral contract, not a freshly-minted protocol) — it is a plateau-app architecture choice (mechanism, not UX-policy). Full grounding in we:reports/2026-06-22-1391-split-analysis.md.
